narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd like—specifically, a 46.9% chance of not passing on the first try. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class that year, which painted a picture of real struggles catching up academically as things got tougher. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, showing those early grades also lagged, suggesting a pattern that worried the model most. Your LSAT score of 27 added to the caution, as it fell short of the benchmarks where others thrive on the bar. Even with your upper-middle family income, which might offer some support, it didn't quite offset the rest in the model's eyes—almost like having resources but not the track record to leverage them fully. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 echoed those concerns, hinting at consistent hurdles in rigorous settings. Being a white male studying full-time didn't shift the tide much here, as the model saw those traits aligning more with the challenging side in similar cases. It's this full mosaic—low deciles dominating the narrative—that led us to pause, not any single flaw, but the combined weight suggesting you'd face steeper odds. You're clearly driven, aiming straight for law, and this isn't the end of your story; many turn things around by targeting those early academic footholds. What if you boosted your deciles through targeted prep or retakes? We've seen applicants like you pivot and succeed—keep pushing, because your potential is there beneath the numbers. Hang in there; law needs voices like yours.
